client.name: RSAF Agile Innovation & Digital
client.subtitle: Defence Technology | Air Force Digital Transformation
client.description: RAiD is the Republic of Singapore Air Force's dedicated digital innovation and agile software development unit, established to modernise RSAF's operational and administrative systems through iterative, user-centred engineering. Operating under the Ministry of Defence (MINDEF), RAiD builds and maintains mission-critical internal platforms, data pipelines, and digital tools that support air operations, logistics, and personnel management. The unit applies commercial-grade software engineering practices — including DevSecOps, cloud-native architecture, and agile delivery — within Singapore's defence environment.

keyFacts.label: Established
keyFacts.desc: Stood up as part of RSAF's broader Digital Transformation agenda

keyFacts.label: Team Size
keyFacts.desc: Mix of uniformed officers, regulars, NSFs, and civilian software engineers

keyFacts.label: Cloud Platform
keyFacts.desc: Singapore Government Commercial Cloud — primary hosting environment

serviceAreas.title: Agile Software Delivery
serviceAreas.desc: Designs, builds and maintains internal RSAF platforms using iterative sprint cycles and modern engineering practices.

serviceAreas.title: Digital Innovation & R&D
serviceAreas.desc: Explores emerging technologies — AI/ML, data analytics, IoT — and pilots their application to air force operational problems.

serviceAreas.title: Platform & DevSecOps Engineering
serviceAreas.desc: Maintains the developer platform, CI/CD pipelines, cloud infrastructure, and security compliance tooling that all product squads build on.

serviceAreas.title: Data Engineering & Analytics
serviceAreas.desc: Builds data pipelines, dashboards, and analytical models that give RSAF commanders and planners operational insight.

serviceAreas.title: Systems Integration
serviceAreas.desc: Connects legacy RSAF systems with modern cloud-native platforms, ensuring interoperability across defence information systems.

serviceAreas.title: User Experience & Product Design
serviceAreas.desc: Embeds UX designers and product managers within delivery squads to ensure tools are intuitive for operational end-users.

priorities.title: Accelerate Internal Product Delivery Velocity
priorities.desc: RAiD teams are expected to deliver production-grade RSAF tools at a pace comparable to commercial startups while meeting defence security requirements. This means compressing sprint-to-deploy cycles, reducing manual review gates, and maintaining quality without slowing release cadence. AI-assisted coding is a key lever being explored to multiply engineering throughput without requiring headcount growth.
priorities.roles: Software Engineers, QA Engineers, DevOps & Platform
priorities.pains: Manual testing bottlenecks, long security scan queues, context-switching across multiple product squads, limited senior engineer bandwidth for code review

priorities.title: Embed DevSecOps Across All Product Squads
priorities.tagline: Security baked in, not bolted on
priorities.desc: As a defence organisation, RAiD must meet stringent IM8 (Instruction Manual 8) and MINDEF cybersecurity policies across every system it operates. The priority is shifting security left so that every engineer owns secure coding, SAST/DAST scans are automated in CI, and compliance evidence is generated continuously rather than assembled at audit time. This reduces last-minute security remediations that delay releases.
priorities.roles: Security Engineers, DevOps & Platform, Software Engineers
priorities.pains: Manual compliance evidence gathering, inconsistent secret management practices, unclear ownership of vulnerabilities across squads, slow security review turnaround before CAB submission

priorities.title: Build a Sustainable Engineering Talent Pipeline
priorities.desc: RAiD operates in a uniquely challenging talent environment: uniformed officers cycle through on 2-3 year postings, NSF software engineers serve for 2 years and then leave, and civilian hires compete with higher-paying tech industry employers. The priority is accelerating onboarding, codifying institutional knowledge, and developing junior engineers to production-level competency faster — so each posting cohort contributes meaningfully before rotating out.
priorities.roles: Tech Leads & Architects, Software Engineers, Product & Planning
priorities.pains: Steep onboarding curve for engineers unfamiliar with defence systems, knowledge loss when officers rotate postings, junior engineers needing close mentorship before contributing independently

priorities.title: Modernise Legacy Mission Systems and Interfaces
priorities.desc: RSAF has a portfolio of older operational systems — some bespoke, some COTS — that require modernisation to support new data flows and user experiences. RAiD is progressively migrating these to cloud-native architectures on GCC, wrapping legacy APIs, and retiring outdated interfaces. This work is complex due to mission-criticality requirements, strict change management, and the need to maintain continuous operations during migration.
priorities.roles: Tech Leads & Architects, Software Engineers, DevOps & Platform
priorities.pains: Undocumented legacy codebases, risk-averse change approval processes, missing test coverage on legacy systems, dependency on external vendors for system knowledge

priorities.title: Scale AI/ML for Operational Decision Support
priorities.tagline: Intelligence at the speed of ops
priorities.desc: RAiD is developing AI and machine learning capabilities to support RSAF planners and operators — including predictive maintenance for aircraft, resource optimisation, and natural language interfaces to operational data. This requires building robust data infrastructure, responsible AI governance, and tooling to validate model outputs in high-stakes defence contexts. The AI tools built must be explainable and auditable for mission-critical use.
priorities.roles: Software Engineers, Tech Leads & Architects, Data Engineers
priorities.pains: Data quality and labelling effort, model validation complexity in regulated environments, shortage of ML engineering expertise within the team, integration complexity with operational legacy systems

capabilities.objectives: Software Engineers at RAiD form the core delivery force across all product squads, building web applications, APIs, data pipelines, and integration layers that power RSAF's internal digital tools. They work in cross-functional squads aligned to RSAF user groups — such as air operations, logistics, or personnel — and own features end-to-end from design to production deployment on GCC. Engineers are expected to maintain high code quality, write automated tests, and participate actively in agile ceremonies.
capabilities.painPoints: Context-switching between multiple squad commitments, limited time for deep work due to ad-hoc operational queries from RSAF users, slow onboarding to existing codebases with sparse documentation, frequent requirement changes driven by shifting operational priorities and posting rotations
capabilities.keyActivities: Feature development, code review, unit and integration testing, sprint planning, technical design, API development, database schema design, PR review

capabilities.objectives: QA Engineers at RAiD are responsible for ensuring that software deployed to RSAF operations meets functional, performance, and security quality bars before it reaches end-users in operational contexts where failures can have mission impact. They design and maintain automated test suites, perform exploratory testing, and manage UAT coordination with RSAF user representatives. Given the sensitivity of RSAF systems, QA also plays a role in verifying compliance with data handling and security requirements.
capabilities.painPoints: Manual regression testing of features across multiple environments, coordinating UAT with operational users who have limited availability due to flying and exercise schedules, test environment instability causing false failures, insufficient test automation coverage inherited from early-stage product development
capabilities.keyActivities: Test planning, automated test suite development, regression testing, UAT facilitation, defect management, performance testing, security testing coordination, environment validation

capabilities.objectives: Tech Leads and Architects at RAiD set the technical direction for product squads and the broader platform, making decisions on architecture patterns, technology selection, and engineering standards that must balance agility with the long-term security and maintainability requirements of a defence organisation. They mentor junior engineers, lead technical design reviews, and interface with MINDEF cybersecurity policy teams to ensure alignment. Senior architects also oversee cross-squad platform decisions and manage technical roadmaps across the RAiD portfolio.
capabilities.painPoints: Balancing hands-on engineering with mentoring and architectural oversight, navigating bureaucratic approval processes for new technology adoption on GCC, knowledge concentration risk when senior engineers rotate postings, difficulty enforcing architecture standards consistently across autonomous squads
capabilities.keyActivities: Architecture design, technical design reviews, engineering standards authorship, technology evaluation, mentoring junior engineers, cross-squad coordination, security policy interpretation, posting handover documentation

capabilities.objectives: The Product and Planning function at RAiD bridges the gap between RSAF operational users and engineering teams, translating complex operational requirements — often articulated by non-technical military users — into clear product roadmaps and well-scoped user stories. Product Managers work with RSAF unit commanders and operations staff to prioritise features by operational impact, manage stakeholder expectations, and ensure that what gets built genuinely solves real mission problems rather than creating technical solutions in search of a problem.
capabilities.painPoints: Difficulty eliciting precise requirements from operational users who communicate in domain-specific military terminology, frequent priority shifts driven by evolving operational tempo and exercises, limited influence over deployment timelines governed by CAB and change management committees, balancing short-term operational requests with long-term platform health
capabilities.keyActivities: User research, requirements gathering, product roadmap management, backlog grooming, stakeholder management, sprint review facilitation, OKR setting and tracking, operational user engagement

capabilities.objectives: The DevOps and Platform Engineering team at RAiD maintains the foundational developer infrastructure that all product squads depend on — including CI/CD pipelines on GitLab, GCC cloud environments (AWS and Azure), Kubernetes clusters, monitoring and observability stacks, and the internal developer portal. They own the deployment pipeline from commit to production and are responsible for ensuring the platform meets MINDEF's stringent availability, security, and audit requirements. They also drive adoption of Infrastructure-as-Code and GitOps practices across the organisation.
capabilities.painPoints: Responding to urgent infrastructure incidents while maintaining planned platform improvement work, managing multiple environment configurations across development, staging, and production on GCC, keeping pace with MINDEF security policy changes that require platform updates, long lead times for cloud resource provisioning through government procurement processes
capabilities.keyActivities: CI/CD pipeline management, cloud infrastructure provisioning, Kubernetes cluster operations, incident response, monitoring and alerting, security patching, Terraform IaC development, developer enablement and onboarding support

capabilities.objectives: Security Engineers at RAiD operate in a uniquely high-stakes environment where defence systems must meet both Singapore Government IM8 cybersecurity standards and MINDEF-specific classified system requirements for Certification & Accreditation. They conduct threat modelling, manage vulnerability programmes, implement SAST/DAST tooling in CI pipelines, and respond to security incidents across the RAiD application portfolio. Security Engineers also advise product squads on secure architecture decisions and prepare documentation for government security audits and C&A submissions.
capabilities.painPoints: High volume of security findings generated by automated scanners requiring manual triage and disposition, coordinating remediation across multiple autonomous product squads with competing sprint priorities, assembling accreditation documentation packages for new system deployments, keeping application dependencies patched in GCC environments with restricted external internet access
capabilities.keyActivities: Threat modelling, SAST/DAST tooling management, vulnerability management and triage, security audit preparation, penetration testing coordination, secure code review, incident response, IM8 compliance tracking, C&A package assembly

workflows.name: Feature Development & Delivery

steps.d: Product Manager collects operational requirement from RSAF user unit; translates military need into a user story with acceptance criteria and data classification assessment
Engineer or Tech Lead writes a design document; reviewed asynchronously by peers and Security Engineer for threat model implications and IM8 alignment
Engineers implement the feature in a 2-week sprint; PR raised for peer code review and automated CI checks including lint, unit tests, and SonarQube SAST scan
QA runs automated regression suite and exploratory testing on staging; UAT session scheduled with RSAF user representatives from the operational unit
Change request submitted to MINDEF Change Advisory Board (CAB); reviewed for security and operational impact before production deployment approval is granted
DevOps triggers deployment via GitLab CI/CD pipeline to GCC production Kubernetes cluster; post-deployment smoke tests run automatically and monitoring alerts confirmed

workflows.friction: CAB review cycle adds 1-2 weeks to release cadence; UAT scheduling with operational users who have primary flying or planning duties is persistently difficult; SAST findings require manual security review before PR approval

workflows.name: Security Compliance & Accreditation

steps.d: Security Engineer maps new system against MINDEF IM8 controls and identifies gaps requiring technical remediation or formal risk acceptance by the approving authority
SAST and DAST scans run continuously in GitLab CI pipeline; findings triaged by Security Engineer weekly for severity classification and false positive removal
Critical and high findings assigned to the engineering squad for remediation; tracked in Jira backlog alongside feature work and prioritised by Security Engineer
External or internal pen test conducted against staging environment; findings documented, severity-rated, and remediated before C&A package is assembled
Security Engineer assembles Certification & Accreditation evidence package including architecture diagrams, scan reports, pen test results, and risk acceptances for submission to approving authority
System receives C&A approval from MINDEF; production deployment authorised for classified or sensitive data processing; certification scheduled for annual renewal review

workflows.friction: Manual assembly of C&A evidence packages is highly labour-intensive and poorly tooled; remediation competes directly with feature delivery for sprint capacity; accreditation approval timelines are unpredictable and can stall releases by weeks

workflows.name: Incident Response

steps.d: Datadog or CloudWatch alert fires, or an RSAF operational user reports a system anomaly via the internal helpdesk ticketing system to the on-call DevOps engineer
On-call DevOps engineer assesses severity using the MINDEF incident classification matrix; creates incident ticket in Jira and notifies the relevant squad and Tech Lead via Teams
Squad investigates Datadog logs, traces, and metrics; applies hotfix branch or rollback via GitLab pipeline to restore service within the defined SLA window
If the incident is security-related or classified P1/P2 severity, it is formally reported to the MINDEF Cybersecurity team within the required notification window per IM8 requirements
Post-incident RCA written by the squad on Confluence within 48 hours; identifies contributing factors, contributing system conditions, and preventive actions to be taken
Preventive actions added to squad Jira backlog with assigned owners; incident reviewed at next squad retrospective and platform team weekly sync for systemic improvements

workflows.friction: On-call coverage is thin given team size relative to the number of production systems; observability tooling coverage is inconsistent across older legacy RSAF systems; MINDEF notification requirements add process overhead during active incidents when speed is critical

workflows.name: New Engineer Onboarding

steps.d: New engineer undergoes MINDEF security screening and vetting process; SAF network account, GCC environment access, and GitLab permissions provisioned only after clearance is granted
DevOps or buddy engineer walks new joiner through developer environment setup, VPN configuration, GCC console access, GitLab onboarding, Jira, and Confluence navigation
Tech Lead assigns an engineer buddy; new joiner reads available architecture docs on Confluence, explores assigned squad's GitLab repositories, and gets the local development environment running
New engineer picks up low-risk tasks from the squad Jira backlog — typically bug fixes or small enhancements — to build familiarity with the codebase, CI/CD workflow, and code review norms
MINDEF cybersecurity awareness training and IM8 induction completed; squad-specific secure coding practices and secret management procedures reviewed with the Security Engineer
Engineer takes on standard sprint load and attends all agile ceremonies; buddy check-ins continue for the first 3 months; Tech Lead assesses readiness for independent feature ownership at 90-day mark

workflows.friction: Security clearance and GCC access provisioning can take 2-4 weeks, delaying productive contribution from day one; documentation is sparse or outdated requiring heavy reliance on tribal knowledge from squad members; engineers rotating from other RSAF units need significant reorientation to agile workflows and commercial development practices

individual.name: Codebase Orientation Time
individual.desc: Engineers joining a new squad or inheriting legacy code spend significant time reading unfamiliar codebases with sparse or outdated documentation on Confluence. This ramp-up period delays productive contribution and creates dependency on senior engineers for context that leaves when they rotate postings.

individual.name: Boilerplate and Repetitive Code
individual.desc: Writing unit tests, API endpoint scaffolding, data model definitions, and database migration scripts is time-consuming but low-cognitive-value work that engineers must complete to meet quality standards. This occupies bandwidth that could be redirected to solving harder operational problems.

individual.name: Security Scan Findings Triage
individual.desc: SonarQube SAST scans generate large volumes of findings — many false positives — that engineers must manually review and disposition before PRs can be approved and merged. This creates recurring friction in the review cycle and is a persistent context-switch away from development work.

individual.name: Documentation Debt
individual.desc: Operational pressure to ship features means documentation is routinely deferred or skipped entirely. Engineers who need to understand a system's design rationale, API contracts, or deployment procedures often find Confluence docs missing, outdated, or contradicting the actual code.

individual.name: Navigating MINDEF Policy Requirements
individual.desc: Engineers regularly encounter IM8 policies, GCC usage guidelines, and data classification rules that constrain how features can be implemented. Interpreting these policies correctly — and knowing when to escalate to the Security Engineer for a ruling — consumes significant time and creates anxiety about inadvertent compliance violations.

individual.name: Environment Configuration Drift
individual.desc: Development, staging, and production GCC environments can diverge in configuration over time, causing bugs that appear only in production and are difficult to reproduce locally. Debugging environment-specific failures is time-consuming and frustrating, particularly when GCC console access is required.

individual.name: Interrupted Deep Work
individual.desc: Engineers are frequently pulled from focused development by ad-hoc requests from RSAF operational users, Teams messages from product teams, and urgent helpdesk escalations from other squads. This fragmentation of focus makes it difficult to maintain the flow state required for complex problem-solving.

individual.name: Posting Rotation Knowledge Loss
individual.desc: When officers or NSF engineers rotate out of their posting, their system knowledge, design rationale, and hard-won institutional context leave with them. The incoming engineer must reconstruct this understanding from incomplete documentation and the fading memories of remaining squad members.

team.name: Change Advisory Board Latency
team.desc: Production deployments require CAB approval, adding 1-2 weeks to release cycles. This creates pressure to batch multiple features into single release windows, increasing deployment risk and slowing feedback loops from operational users who need the features to do their jobs.

team.name: UAT Coordination with Operational Users
team.desc: RSAF operational users who participate in UAT have primary duties — flying, ops planning, logistics — and limited scheduled availability for testing sessions. Scheduling, conducting, and getting formal sign-off from UAT participants is a persistent coordination overhead that delays feature acceptance and release.

team.name: Inconsistent Engineering Standards Across Squads
team.desc: With multiple autonomous squads operating in parallel, code style, testing standards, and architectural patterns diverge over time. Platform-level changes or cross-squad features surface these inconsistencies, creating integration friction, rework, and difficult code review conversations.

team.name: Sprint Capacity Fragmentation
team.desc: Each sprint, squad capacity is eroded by security remediation tasks, helpdesk escalations, tech debt paydown, and mandatory SAF administrative duties such as safety inspections and mandatory training, leaving consistently less time for planned feature delivery than nominal velocity suggests.

team.name: Legacy System Integration Complexity
team.desc: New RAiD products frequently need to interface with older RSAF systems that have undocumented APIs, unreliable behaviour, or vendor-controlled codebases. Integration work routinely takes significantly longer than estimated and is a recurring source of late-breaking defects discovered only during UAT.

organisation.name: Engineering Talent Retention
organisation.desc: RAiD competes for software engineering talent against Singapore's tech industry, which offers significantly higher total compensation. Civilian engineers require active retention efforts and career development investment, while uniformed engineers cycle through postings on fixed terms, creating a structural knowledge loss challenge that compounds over years.

organisation.name: Technology Adoption Governance Lag
organisation.desc: Adopting new tools, frameworks, or cloud services requires approval through MINDEF procurement and security review processes that can take many months. This lag between commercial technology maturity and organisational adoption means RAiD sometimes begins building on platforms that are approaching deprecation by the time they are formally approved.

organisation.name: Institutional Knowledge Preservation
organisation.desc: The combination of posting rotations, NSF turnover, and a limited documentation culture means RAiD faces a persistent and growing risk of losing critical system knowledge. There is no systematic knowledge management practice capable of capturing design decisions, operational context, and system rationale at scale across the entire portfolio.

organisation.name: Demonstrating Software Engineering Value
organisation.desc: Demonstrating the value of software engineering investment to SAF senior leadership — who are primarily operational commanders — is difficult. Standard engineering metrics such as DORA metrics and defect rates do not translate easily into operational outcomes, making it hard to secure resources, headcount approvals, and priority alignment.

organisation.name: Cross-Unit Requirements Alignment
organisation.desc: RAiD builds products for multiple RSAF units with different operational priorities, technical literacy levels, and urgency profiles. Balancing competing demands across user groups without a clear organisational prioritisation framework leads to roadmap conflicts, perceptions of favouritism, and underserved stakeholder communities.

technology.m365Status: RAiD operates within Singapore Government's digital ecosystem using Microsoft 365 under MINDEF's enterprise licensing for unclassified collaboration and productivity. Teams and SharePoint serve as primary internal communication and document management platforms, though classified communications and data are handled through separate secured government systems outside the M365 environment.

platforms.name: GCC (Government Commercial Cloud)
platforms.desc: Singapore Government's secured cloud hosting environment on AWS and Azure; primary production hosting platform for all RAiD applications, with network segmentation and access controls meeting MINDEF requirements

platforms.name: GitLab (Self-Hosted)
platforms.desc: RAiD's primary version control and CI/CD platform, self-hosted on GCC; used for all source code management, merge request reviews, automated pipeline execution, and container registry

platforms.name: SHIP-HATS
platforms.desc: Singapore Government's Secure Hybrid Integrated Pipeline – Hosted Automated Tools; government-managed DevSecOps platform providing compliant CI/CD, code scanning, and security tooling that RAiD integrates with for regulated software delivery

platforms.name: Kubernetes on GCC
platforms.desc: Container orchestration platform running on GCC; used by the Platform Team for deploying and scaling RAiD's microservices and web applications across development, staging, and production namespaces

platforms.name: Terraform
platforms.desc: Infrastructure-as-Code tool used by the DevOps team to provision and manage all GCC cloud resources in a version-controlled, auditable, and repeatable manner aligned to GitOps principles

platforms.name: SonarQube
platforms.desc: Static application security testing (SAST) tool integrated into GitLab CI pipelines; scans every pull request for code quality issues and security vulnerabilities as part of the mandatory pre-merge gate

platforms.name: Datadog
platforms.desc: Observability and monitoring platform for application performance monitoring, log aggregation, distributed tracing, and alerting across all RAiD GCC production environments

platforms.name: Jira
platforms.desc: Agile project management and issue tracking tool used across all product squads for backlog management, sprint planning, defect tracking, and security remediation task management

platforms.name: Confluence
platforms.desc: Internal wiki and documentation platform used for architecture decision records, runbooks, onboarding guides, and project documentation; the primary institutional knowledge repository

platforms.name: SGTS (Singapore Government Tech Stack)
platforms.desc: Suite of shared government engineering services including SingPass authentication integration, Notify.gov.sg for government notifications, and Form.gov.sg for data collection — used by RAiD products where applicable

glossary.d: RSAF Agile Innovation & Digital — the RSAF unit responsible for digital transformation and agile software development within the Singapore Air Force
Republic of Singapore Air Force — one of the three services of the Singapore Armed Forces; RAiD's parent organisation and primary end-user of the software it builds
Singapore Armed Forces — Singapore's military comprising the Army, Republic of Singapore Navy (RSN), and Republic of Singapore Air Force (RSAF)
Ministry of Defence — the Singapore government ministry responsible for the SAF; sets policy, procurement governance, and cybersecurity standards for all defence digital systems
Commanding Officer — the military commander of RAiD; holds overall accountability for the unit's mission, personnel, and operational output
National Serviceman — a full-time national service personnel serving a mandatory 2-year conscription; RAiD employs NSFs as software engineers through the specialist technical national service pathway
A professional full-time SAF serviceman who has signed on beyond National Service; typically holds mid-to-senior engineering or leadership roles within RAiD on multi-year career tracks

The assignment of a uniformed officer or NCO to a specific unit or role for a defined tenure, typically 2-3 years; posting rotations are a primary driver of knowledge loss and onboarding overhead at RAiD

Change Advisory Board — the MINDEF governance body that reviews and approves production changes to RSAF systems; a mandatory gating step in RAiD's release workflow that adds 1-2 weeks to deployment cycles
Officer Commanding — the commander of a company-level subunit within RAiD, typically a Captain or Major, responsible for a cluster of product squads or a functional domain
A cross-functional agile delivery team at RAiD comprising software engineers, a QA engineer, a product manager, and a tech lead, aligned to a specific RSAF product or operational domain
The DevOps and Platform Engineering squad at RAiD that maintains shared CI/CD infrastructure, GCC environments, Kubernetes clusters, and developer tooling used by all product squads
Agile Centre of Excellence — the function within RAiD responsible for coaching squads on agile practices, facilitating retrospectives, and evolving the engineering culture
Government Commercial Cloud — Singapore's secured cloud hosting environment on AWS and Azure, used by government agencies including RAiD for all production workloads meeting MINDEF security standards

Secure Hybrid Integrated Pipeline – Hosted Automated Tools — Singapore Government's managed DevSecOps platform providing compliant CI/CD, code scanning, and security tooling to government software teams

Singapore Government Tech Stack — the suite of shared technology services (SingPass authentication, Notify.gov.sg, Form.gov.sg) that government developers integrate with to avoid building commodity capabilities

Instruction Manual 8 — the Singapore Government's cybersecurity policy framework mandating security controls for all government IT systems; every RAiD product must demonstrate IM8 compliance to receive C&A approval

Certification & Accreditation — the formal MINDEF security approval process required before a system can process classified or sensitive data in production; involves pen testing, evidence assembly, and sign-off from the approving authority
Static Application Security Testing — automated source code scanning for security vulnerabilities; integrated into RAiD's GitLab CI pipelines via SonarQube as a mandatory pre-merge quality gate
Dynamic Application Security Testing — runtime vulnerability scanning of deployed applications; run against RAiD staging environments as part of the security compliance workflow before C&A submission

Infrastructure-as-Code — the practice of managing all GCC cloud infrastructure through version-controlled Terraform configuration files; mandated by RAiD's Platform Team for auditability and reproducibility
An operational model where infrastructure and deployment configurations are managed through Git as the single source of truth; adopted by RAiD's Platform Team for all GCC environment management
Singapore's national digital identity system; RAiD integrates SingPass authentication for RSAF applications requiring verified identity of servicemen or civilian staff accessing sensitive systems
Objectives and Key Results — the goal-setting framework used by RAiD product squads to align quarterly engineering priorities to measurable RSAF operational outcomes

User Acceptance Testing — the final validation phase where RSAF operational users test new features in a staging environment and provide formal sign-off before production deployment is requested at CAB
A 2-week development cycle used by RAiD squads; each sprint begins with planning from the Jira backlog and ends with a review demo presented to RSAF stakeholders
Root Cause Analysis — a post-incident document written by the squad on Confluence within 48 hours of a production incident, identifying contributing factors and preventive actions

The practice of integrating security controls and testing into every phase of the software development lifecycle; a strategic priority for RAiD to meet MINDEF compliance requirements without slowing delivery

A software system directly supporting RSAF operational activities such as air operations planning, mission debrief, or aircraft maintenance tracking; subject to the highest C&A security and availability standards
Operational Tempo — the pace and intensity of RSAF flying and operational activities; directly affects user availability for UAT sessions and product feedback cycles, particularly during exercises

DevOps Research and Assessment metrics (deployment frequency, lead time for changes, mean time to restore, change failure rate) used by RAiD's Platform Team to benchmark and improve engineering delivery performance

A system processing information under Singapore's security classification scheme (Restricted, Confidential, Secret, Top Secret); requires enhanced C&A controls and network segmentation beyond standard IM8 requirements
RAiD's preferred architectural pattern for new GCC-hosted applications, where systems are decomposed into independently deployable services communicating via well-defined REST or gRPC APIs

Accumulated shortcuts and suboptimal design decisions in RAiD's codebases that increase future maintenance cost; managing tech debt is a recurring squad priority that competes with feature delivery for sprint capacity

brandColors.primary: #0066CC — RAiD's primary brand blue, a vibrant medium blue consistent with RSAF's institutional identity and used for primary buttons, logo accents, and interactive elements across defence.gov.sg digital properties
brandColors.accent: #00B4D8 — a cyan-teal accent colour used for innovation highlights, icon fills, and secondary interactive elements, contrasting with the primary navy-blue to convey RAiD's forward-looking digital identity

useCases.name: Write the implementation intent before opening the codebase — feature brief in Jira for RAiD
useCases.pain: Context-switching between multiple squad commitments, limited time for deep work due to ad-hoc operational queries from RSAF users, slow onboarding to existing codebases with sparse documentation, frequent requirement changes driven by shifting operational priorities and posting rotations
useCases.prompt:
I am a Software Engineers at RAiD about to implement:

"Feature development"

Challenge: Context-switching between multiple squad commitments, limited time for deep work due to ad-hoc operational queries from RSAF users, slow onboarding to existing codebases with sparse documentation, frequent requirement changes driven by shifting operational priorities and posting rotations

Before I touch the codebase or create a Jira ticket, help me write a tight implementation intent brief:

1. Outcome — what exactly must be true when this is done, in one sentence?
2. Technical acceptance criteria — 3–5 conditions that automated tests can verify
3. Scope boundary — what files, services, or APIs are IN scope vs. OUT?
4. Likely failure point — where does this type of change typically break at RAiD?
5. Pre-conditions — what must exist (API contracts, migrations, feature flags) before I start?

Output: a paste-ready Jira story description. Do not start implementation.
useCases.inputs: Jira story template; stakeholder request or existing ticket; any relevant API contracts or designs; compliance constraints at RAiD
useCases.metric: Rework from scope misalignment reduced by ~30%; implementation starts with clear acceptance criteria (BCG/Harvard "Jagged Frontier": 25–40% speed gain on well-specified tasks)
useCases.guardrails: Review with product owner or Tech Lead before coding; flag any acceptance criterion that depends on a third-party decision not yet made

useCases.name: Implement a RAiD feature end-to-end with Claude Code — code, tests, and GitLab MR in one agent run
useCases.pain: Context-switching between multiple squad commitments, limited time for deep work due to ad-hoc operational queries from RSAF users, slow onboarding to existing codebases with sparse documentation, frequent requirement changes driven by shifting operational priorities and posting rotations
useCases.prompt:
# In your project directory:
claude

# Paste this task into Claude Code:
I am a Software Engineers at RAiD. Implement the following feature end-to-end.

Feature: [Paste your Jira story title and acceptance criteria here]

This codebase uses GCC, GitLab, SHIP-HATS, Kubernetes on GCC. Act as my engineering agent:

Step 1 — Explore the codebase: find the relevant modules, existing patterns for this type of change, and related tests. Report what you find before touching anything.

Step 2 — Propose your approach: file-by-file plan, any new dependencies, and how you will test it. Wait for my go-ahead.

Step 3 — Implement completely: feature code + unit tests + any config or migration changes. Follow the patterns from Step 1.

Step 4 — Write the GitLab MR description: what changed, why, how to test it locally, and review notes for the Tech Lead.

Constraints:
- Current blocker to address: Context-switching between multiple squad commitments, limited time for deep work due to ad-hoc operational queries from RSAF users, slow onboarding to existing codebases with sparse documentation, frequent requirement changes driven by shifting operational priorities and posting rotations
- Do not modify files outside the scope of this feature
- All new logic must have unit test coverage

Do not write any code until I confirm the approach in Step 2.
useCases.inputs: Jira story with acceptance criteria; any related existing code or API contracts; branch name
useCases.metric: Feature implementation ~55% faster than solo (GitHub Copilot controlled study, N=95, P=.0017 — task time cut from 2h41m to 1h11m on comparable coding task); consistent GitLab MR quality
useCases.guardrails: Review all diffs before raising the MR; confirm the approach at Step 2 before Claude writes any code; run the full GitLab pipeline before merging

useCases.name: Run parallel Claude Code worktrees at RAiD — deliver two Software Engineers tasks simultaneously in separate GitLab branches
useCases.pain: Context-switching between multiple squad commitments, limited time for deep work due to ad-hoc operational queries from RSAF users, slow onboarding to existing codebases with sparse documentation, frequent requirement changes driven by shifting operational priorities and posting rotations
useCases.prompt:
# Set up two separate git worktrees — one per task:
git worktree add ../raid-task-A <branch-name-A>
git worktree add ../raid-task-B <branch-name-B>

# Open a separate terminal and start Claude Code in each worktree:
cd ../raid-task-A && claude

useCases.metric: ~75% of active engineering time converted to passive review (Salesforce/Cursor: 85% effort reduction on test coverage; Anthropic 100k conversations: 80% average time reduction on autonomous tasks); REVIEW.md ready at morning standup
useCases.guardrails: Review REVIEW.md and all diffs before raising the GitLab MR; run the full GitLab pipeline; validate key assumptions; never deploy to RAiD production without human sign-off
